content.title: 2018 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline on the Evaluation and Management of Patients With Bradycardia and Cardiac Conduction Delay
content.description: A report of the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association Task Force on Clinical Practice Guidelines and the Heart Rhythm Society.
content.collaboration: Developed in collaboration with the American Association for Thoracic Surgery, the Pediatric & Congenital Electrophysiology Society, and the Society of Thoracic Surgeons.
content.endorsement: Endorsed by the American Association for Thoracic Surgery, the Pediatric & Congenital Electrophysiology Society, and the Society of Thoracic Surgeons.

content.Title: 2018 Bradycardia Guideline
content.Task Force Members: Led by Glenn N. Levine, MD, FACC, FAHA and includes a group of medical professionals specialized in cardiology.
content.Purpose: Guidelines for the management of bradycardia and cardiac conduction delay.
content.Structure: Includes sections on methodology, evidence review, clinical manifestation, evaluation, and management.
Key Sections: Clinical Manifestations of Bradycardia and Conduction Disorders

content.Breathing disorders and nocturnal bradyc: Treatment of sleep apnea may reduce frequency of arrhythmias and offer cardiovascular benefits. Screening for sleep apnea in the presence of nocturnal bradycardias is advised.
content.Left bundle branch block: Increases risk of structural heart disease and helps diagnose left ventricular systolic dysfunction. Echocardiography is recommended for initial screening.
content.Sinus node dysfunction: No specific minimum heart rate or pause duration is established for permanent pacing. Temporal correlation between symptoms and bradycardia is important.
content.Atrioventricular block: Permanent pacing recommended for high-grade/complete block. Other blocks require specific symptoms for consideration.
content.Left ventricular ejection fraction: Patients with 36%-50% fraction and atrioventricular block should consider permanent pacing. Preference given to physiologic ventricular activation techniques.
content.Conduction system abnormalities: Recommendations on procedure surveillance and pacemaker implant post valve replacement.
content.Shared decision-making: Emphasis on patient-centered care and decision-making for bradycardia with pacemaker indication.
content.Emerging pacing technologies: His bundle pacing, transcatheter leadless pacing systems, require further investigation.
content.Preamble: Clinical guidelines provide recommendations based on evidence, aimed at enhancing patient care and aligning with patient interests through shared decision-making.

introduction.methodology_and_evidence_review: The guidelines are evidence-based, with an extensive review of literature from January 2017 to September 2017. The databases used include PubMed, EMBASE, and others relevant to healthcare research and quality. Key search words include AV block, bradycardia, bundle branch block, conduction disturbance, left bundle branch block, loop recorder, pauses, permanent pacemaker, sick sinus syndrome, sinus node dysfunction, and temporary pacemaker.
introduction.guideline_changes: Modifications have been made to enhance user-friendliness, presented in a modular knowledge chunk format. The complete version is available online.

content.organization_of_the_writing_committee: Composed of experts such as cardiac electrophysiologists and cardiologists. It included representatives from ACC, AHA, HRS, AATS, PACES, and STS.
content.document_review_and_approval: The document was reviewed and approved for publication by the governing bodies such as the ACC, AHA, and HRS; endorsed by other related societies.

content.Purpose: The guideline provides guidance to clinicians for managing patients with bradycardia or related symptoms, associated with bradycardia or cardiac conduction system disorders.
content.Supersedes: 2008 and 2012 ACCF/AHA/HRS guidelines for pacemaker recommendations.
content.Target Audience: General internists, family physicians, emergency physicians, anesthesiologists, surgeons, cardiologists, and arrhythmia specialists.
content.Age Focus: Adult population (>18 years), with some recommendations for pediatric patients.
content.Scope: Focus on practical clinical evaluation and management of bradycardia and cardiac conduction disorders.
Objectives: Describe the clinical significance of bradycardia.
Address inherited/acquired disorders of sinus node and other conducting tissues.
Delineate clinical presentation and evaluation approach for bradycardia.
Evaluate evidence for diagnostic testing modalities.
Define evidence base for treatment modalities including device-based therapies.
content.Recommendations: Designated with class of recommendation (COR) and level of evidence (LOE).

content.Title: Associated Guidelines and Related References
content.Summary: This clinical guideline provides a detailed list of publications and organizations related to various cardiovascular conditions and their management. It includes guidelines for managing ventricular arrhythmias, syncope, stable ischemic heart disease, atrial fibrillation, and postoperative cardiovascular evaluation. The document also includes expert consensus statements on various topics like cardiac device management, MRI imaging in cardiology, and genetic testing for cardiomyopathies.
Key Points: Guidelines are provided by multiple organizations including ACC/AHA/HRS, AHA, ACCF, and others.
Conditions addressed: Ventricular arrhythmias, sudden cardiac death, syncope, ischemic heart disease, atrial fibrillation, and more.
Expert consensus statements on cardiac devices, pacing and resynchronization therapy, and genetic testing.
Discussion on epidemiology and definitions of bradycardia and conduction disorders, highlighting the impact of aging and degenerative changes on conduction.

Recommendation Classes.Class III No Benefit: Moderate evidence for no benefit (Benefit = Risk or ≤ Risk)

Levels of Evidence.Level C-EO: Expert opinion based on clinical experience

content.Clinical Manifestations: Bradycardia can vary from insidious symptoms to episodes of frank syncope. It is classified into SND (Sinus Node Dysfunction) and atrioventricular block categories. The manifestations depend on electrophysiologic variations, ventricular rates, and medication effects. SND may present with ectopic atrial rhythm, intermittent pauses, or ventricular escape. Atrioventricular block symptoms depend on whether the block is fixed or intermittent.
content.Definitions: Bradycardia is defined as a heart rate <60 bpm in adults. Clinically significant pauses are >3 seconds. Chronotropic incompetence is defined by failure to reach expected heart rates during exertion, often related to the age-predicted maximal heart rate.
content.Conditions Addressed: Bradycardia, conduction disorders, sinus node dysfunction, atrioventricular block.

Definition or Description: Ectopic atrial bradycardia: Atrial depolarization attributable to an atrial pacemaker other than the sinus node with a rate <50 bpm
Sinoatrial exit block: Evidence that blocked conduction between the sinus node and adjacent atrial tissue is present.
Sinus pause: Sinus node depolarizes >3 s after the last atrial depolarization
Sinus node arrest: No evidence of sinus node depolarization
Tachycardia-bradycardia ('tachy-brady') syndrome: Sinus bradycardia, ectopic atrial bradycardia, or sinus pause alternating with periods of abnormal atrial tachycardia, atrial flutter, or AF.
Chronotropic incompetence: Inability of the heart to increase its rate commensurate with increased activity or demand.
Iatrogenic: Related to medical examination or treatment.
First-degree atrioventricular block: Associated with 1:1 atrioventricular conduction and a PR interval >200 ms.
Second-degree atrioventricular block: P waves with a constant rate (<100 bpm)
Mobitz type I: P waves with a constant rate (<100 bpm) with a periodic single nonconducted P wave
Mobitz type II: P waves with a constant rate (<100 bpm) with a periodic single nonconducted P wave with a given block pattern.
2:1 atrioventricular block: P waves with a constant rate
Advanced, high-grade, or high-degree atrioventricular block: >2 consecutive P waves at a constant physiologic rate
Incomplete RBBB: Same QRS morphology criteria as complete RBBB but with a QRS duration between 110 and 119 ms
Complete LBBB: QRS duration ≥120 ms in adults

content.Synopsis: The history and physical examination remain the foundation for clinically evaluating patients with potential bradycardia or conduction disorders. This process is particularly critical for patients with structural heart disease.
content.Recommendation: In patients with suspected bradycardia or conduction disorders, a comprehensive history and physical examination should be performed.
content.Clinical Manifestations: Manifestations depend on the underlying cause. Patients with isolated right bundle branch block may be asymptomatic. Patients with left bundle branch block may experience heart failure.
content.Potential Causes: Bradycardia may be due to conduction disorders or systemic illnesses, often complicated by vagal syncope.
content.Evaluation: The evaluation should address the frequency, timing, duration, and triggers of symptoms, involving a review of medications and lifestyle factors.

content.figure: Evaluation of Bradycardia and Conduction Disease Algorithm
key_steps: Patient evaluation begins if symptoms suggestive of bradycardia or conduction disorder are present.
Comprehensive history and physical examination is recommended (Class I).
ECG (Class I) and directed blood testing (Class IIa) are advised depending on findings.
Algorithm branches based on conduction disorders (SND, AV Block, non-diagnostic) with respective diagnostic pathways.
Exercise and ambulatory ECG testing are recommended based on symptoms (Classes I, IIa).
Careful clinical examination including carotid sinus massage as stroke risks with carotid sinus syndrome are highlighted.

recommendation_classes.Class I: General acceptance and usage for these practices.

additional_notes.Autonomic regulation disorders are noted: logy is noted to be essential in ensuring correct diagnosis of bradycardia phenotypes.

content.note: Colors correspond to Class of Recommendation. Sinus pauses, sinus bradycardia, junctional rhythm, ectopic atrial rhythm (all with heart rates <50 bpm) while awake.
